currentState: Open rupture widened into strategic divergence. Chapter 18 sends Arthas north and Uther west, making their break geographic as well as moral.
powerDynamic: Arthas keeps initiative, chosen followers, and forward momentum. Uther keeps witness, legitimacy, and the duty to carry the warning back toward Capital City.

physicalDynamics.pattern: Uther stands opposite Arthas at the command table, the old teacher literally braced against the same map the prince is already trying to seize with his hands.
Outside the tent their dynamic shifts from formal command argument to tired truth-telling beside a column already in motion.

The most telling body language in chapter 15 is displacement: Arthas remains fixed at the map while Uther moves between triage, warning, and the horse line, trying to save what can still be saved around the prince’s momentum.
Uther steps forward with his hammer low while Arthas answers from the same stillness, making the whole split happen through posture before the men behind them choose sides.
The horse wheel is the chapter’s decisive gesture between them: Uther turning away with his own people while Arthas rides into the city without slowing.
Chapter 18 turns the split into geography: one road west toward the kingdom's center, one sea-lane north toward the next campaign.

nameUsage.arthasCallsUther: The old direct-name habit now lands as pure dismissal rather than shared history.
nameUsage.utherCallsArthas: Still bare Arthas, but chapter 17 turns the familiarity into accusation and last appeal at once.

history.event: Sylvanas’s briefing in chapter 11 gives Uther a new battlefield to name, but the underlying flaw he sees in Arthas is clearly older than this room.
Uther openly warns Arthas not to confuse motion with sufficient understanding, and Arthas rides anyway.
Uther challenges Arthas’s refusal to wait, then reframes the dispute around lane rather than halt when it becomes clear the main march will not brake.
Arthas accepts the side attempt without changing his own tempo, confirming that mentor and student now operate beside one another more than with one another.
Uther publicly refuses the Culling instead of contesting Arthas only in private counsel.
Arthas tells him to take every knight and soldier who still knows the difference, effectively acknowledging the split as irreparable in the moment.
The relay confirms the aftermath line has hardened: Arthas is sailing for Northrend while Uther rides west for Capital City.

trajectory: Mentor warning -> parallel answers -> chapter 17’s open command-chain fracture -> chapter 18’s full directional split.

stateHistory.currentState: Mentor and prince locked in the oldest argument between them: Arthas treats conviction as virtue, and Uther recognizes the disaster hiding inside that certainty.
Mentor and prince now split between one march and two answers. Arthas refuses to brake; Uther responds by backing the one smaller lane he still thinks may save lives.
The moral break becomes a kingdom-scale divergence. Arthas drives north into the campaign he has chosen; Uther rides west carrying the warning home.
